1:
"Paul, and Silvanus, and Timotheus, unto the church of the Thessalonians which is in God the Father and in the Lord Jesus Christ: Grace be unto you, and peace, from God our Father, and the Lord Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the greeting from the three to the church in God and Christ. On "Paul, and Silvanus, and Timotheus," the grammatical voices note the senders. Barnes and Robertson note that Paul joins with himself the two who had been with him in founding the church: "Silvanus (Silas) and Timotheus (Timothy)" — his fellow-labourers at Thessalonica (Acts 17), associated here on a footing of near-equality, Paul not even adding his apostolic title.

On "unto the church of the Thessalonians which is in God the Father and in the Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the address. Barnes and Calvin note the distinctive phrase: the church "in God the Father and in the Lord Jesus Christ" — the church's very being and life are rooted in God and Christ; the Father and the Son named together as the sphere of its existence.

On "grace be unto you, and peace," the latin-fathers Tertullian and grammatical-historical note the benediction: the customary blessing — "grace (free favour) and peace (its fruit), from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" — Father and Son the joint source. The verse displays the greeting from the three to the church in God and Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul joins "Silvanus (Silas) and Timotheus (Timothy)," his fellow-founders, on near-equal footing, omitting his apostolic title (Barnes, Robertson); the church "in God the Father and in the Lord Jesus Christ," its being rooted in God and Christ together (Barnes, Calvin); "grace and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ," Father and Son the joint source (Tertullian, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the greeting. The verse marks the senders: Paul, Silvanus, Timothy. The verse displays the church: in God and Christ. The verse displays the blessing: grace and peace. The verse displays the source: Father and Son. The greeting displays the three saluting the church rooted in God. The verse displays the senders: three. The verse displays the church: in God. The verse displays the grace: invoked. The greeting from the three to the church in God and Christ displays the opening of Paul's earliest letter: Paul, Silas, and Timothy salute the Thessalonian church — a church whose very life is rooted in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ — invoking grace and peace from both. The verse displays the apostolic greeting, opening into thanksgiving (v.2).
2:
"We give thanks to God always for you all, making mention of you in our prayers;" The brief verse notes Paul's constant thanksgiving and prayer for them all. The grammatical voices note the gratitude. Barnes and Robertson note Paul opens, as is his custom, with thanksgiving: "we give thanks to God always (constantly) for you all" — gratitude to God for the whole Thessalonian church, embracing every member ("you all").

On "always," the witnesses note the constancy. Barnes and Calvin note that Paul's thanksgiving is continual — not occasional but a settled, ongoing gratitude for the grace God had wrought in them; their faith gave him perpetual cause for thanks.

On "making mention of you in our prayers," the puritan Henry notes the intercession: Paul's thanksgiving was joined with prayer — "making mention of you in our prayers" — remembering the Thessalonians by name before God, both thanking and asking on their behalf. The verse displays Paul's constant thanksgiving and prayer for them.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul opens with thanksgiving — "we give thanks to God always (constantly) for you all," embracing the whole church (Barnes, Robertson); the thanksgiving continual, a settled gratitude for God's grace in them (Barnes, Calvin); joined with intercession, "making mention of you in our prayers," remembering them by name before God (Henry). The verse displays thanksgiving and prayer. The verse marks the thanks: always. The verse displays the scope: you all. The verse displays the constancy: continual. The verse displays the prayer: mention of you. The thanksgiving displays Paul grateful and praying for them. The verse displays the thanks: to God. The verse displays the all: included. The verse displays the prayers: for them. Paul's constant thanksgiving and prayer for them all display the warmth of his pastoral love: he gives thanks to God continually for the whole Thessalonian church, and joins his gratitude with constant intercession, remembering them by name in his prayers. The verse displays the apostle's thankful prayer, grounded in their faith, love, and hope (v.3). What marks this opening, and indeed the whole letter, is the entire absence of rebuke, for unlike the troubled churches of Corinth or Galatia the Thessalonians gave their founder almost unmixed cause for gratitude; and so his prayers for them are not the anxious pleadings of a pastor alarmed for his flock but the glad thanksgivings of a father rejoicing in children who walk in the truth, a gratitude so constant that it rose to God on their behalf every time they came to mind.
3:
"Remembering without ceasing your work of faith, and labour of love, and patience of hope in our Lord Jesus Christ, in the sight of God and our Father;" The witnesses note the threefold grace remembered: work of faith, labour of love, patience of hope. On "your work of faith," the grammatical voices note the first. Barnes and Robertson note the famous triad of Christian graces: "work of faith (the work that faith produces, faith proving itself in action)" — a living faith that works.

On "labour of love," the witnesses note the second. Barnes and Calvin note "labour of love (the toil that love prompts, love that spends itself in effort and self-denial)" — love not in word only but in laborious service.

On "patience of hope in our Lord Jesus Christ," the eastern Cyril and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the third: "patience (steadfast endurance) of hope in our Lord Jesus Christ" — the perseverance that hope inspires, the steadfast waiting for Christ that sustains under trial. The triad — faith, love, hope (cf. 1 Corinthians 13:13) — each shown by its fruit, all "in the sight of God." The verse displays the threefold grace remembered.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the famous triad — "work of faith (faith proving itself in action)," "labour of love (love that toils and spends itself)," and "patience of hope in our Lord Jesus Christ (the endurance hope inspires, steadfast waiting for Christ)" (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin); faith, love, hope each shown by its fruit, all "in the sight of God" (Cyril, Wesley). The verse displays the triad. The verse marks the faith: its work. The verse displays the love: its labour. The verse displays the hope: its patience. The verse displays the object: in our Lord Jesus Christ. The triad displays the graces shown by their fruit. The verse displays the faith: working. The verse displays the love: labouring. The verse displays the hope: enduring. The threefold grace remembered — work of faith, labour of love, patience of hope — displays the living religion of the Thessalonians: their faith worked, their love laboured, and their hope endured steadfastly in the Lord Jesus Christ — the great triad of Christian graces, each proven by its fruit in the sight of God. The verse displays the graces of the model church, rooted in their election (v.4).
4:
"Knowing, brethren beloved, your election of God." The witnesses note the assurance of their election of God — the crux of election. On "brethren beloved," the grammatical voices note the affection. Barnes and Robertson note the tender address: "brethren beloved (of God)" — Ellicott and Robertson noting the Greek means "brethren beloved by God," the perfect tense marking God's long-continued love, proven by their election.

On "your election of God," interpreters divide. The Reformed Gill and Calvin take "election" as the eternal, gratuitous choice of God unto everlasting life, made before the world began, springing from God's sovereign will, not from foreseen faith or merit — manifested by its marks (v.5); Calvin: gratuitous election must hold the first place, "from which faith itself flows." The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley, with Ellicott, take "election" as a gracious admission into present gospel privileges (the Gentiles called in, as the Jews were once chosen), conditional and able to be forfeited — Clarke expressly: "no irrespective, unconditional, eternal, and personal election to everlasting glory is meant." The grammatical-historical Barnes takes a middle path: the church as such (not necessarily every member) gave evidence of its election, rejecting Clarke's Gentiles-for-Jews reading since the church included Jews.

The witnesses note the knowledge: Paul "knew" their election by its evidences — the power of the gospel among them and the fruit it bore. The verse displays the assurance of their election of God.

The witnesses agree the substance: Paul, addressing them as "beloved of God," knew their "election of God" — not by direct revelation but by its evidences, the power of the gospel among them and the fruit it bore (v.5, Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). But the nature of the election divides them: Gill and Calvin hold it the eternal, gratuitous choice unto salvation, from which faith flows; Clarke, Wesley, and Ellicott hold it a gracious admission into present gospel privileges, conditional and forfeitable, denying any unconditional election to glory; Barnes holds the church as such evidenced its election, without affirming it of every member. The verse displays the election crux. The verse marks the address: brethren beloved. The verse displays the knowledge: knowing your election. The verse displays the debate: eternal choice or present privilege. The verse displays the evidence: the gospel's power. The assurance displays Paul knowing their election by its fruit. The verse displays the beloved: of God. The verse displays the election: known. The verse displays the marks: evident. The assurance of their election of God displays Paul's confidence and a doctrinal nerve: he knew the Thessalonians were beloved and chosen of God — read as the eternal unconditional choice unto salvation (Gill, Calvin), or as a conditional election to present gospel privileges that may be forfeited (Clarke, Wesley, Ellicott) — knowing it by the power of the gospel and the fruit it bore. The verse displays their election, evidenced by the gospel's power (v.5).
5:
"For our gospel came not unto you in word only, but also in power, and in the Holy Ghost, and in much assurance; as ye know what manner of men we were among you for your sake." The witnesses note the gospel coming in power and the Holy Ghost, not in word only. On "our gospel came not unto you in word only, but also in power," the grammatical voices note the demonstration. Barnes and Robertson note the evidence of their election (v.4): "our gospel came not unto you in word only (mere speech, bare preaching) but also in power" — accompanied by mighty effect, whether of miracles or, more likely, the converting power on hearts.

On "and in the Holy Ghost, and in much assurance," the witnesses note the Spirit's work. Barnes and Calvin note that the gospel came "in the Holy Ghost (by the Spirit's power and presence) and in much assurance (full conviction, deep persuasion)" — the Spirit gave it efficacy and produced firm, settled conviction in the hearers.

On "as ye know what manner of men we were among you," the Reformed Calvin notes the witness of conduct: the Thessalonians knew "what manner of men we were among you for your sake" — Paul's own life and conduct among them confirmed the gospel; his integrity was part of its power. The verse displays the gospel coming in power and the Holy Ghost.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gospel "came not in word only (bare preaching) but also in power," with mighty converting effect (Barnes, Robertson); "in the Holy Ghost and in much assurance (full conviction)," the Spirit giving efficacy and firm persuasion (Barnes, Calvin); "what manner of men we were among you," Paul's conduct confirming the gospel (Calvin). The verse displays the gospel's power. The verse marks the negative: not in word only. The verse displays the power: mighty effect. The verse displays the Spirit: the Holy Ghost. The verse displays the assurance: full conviction. The gospel displays its coming with the Spirit's power. The verse displays the word: not bare. The verse displays the power: present. The verse displays the conviction: deep. The gospel coming in power and the Holy Ghost, not in word only, displays the evidence of their election: the gospel reached the Thessalonians not as mere speech but with converting power, in the Holy Ghost and in full conviction — confirmed by the manner of life Paul lived among them. The verse displays the gospel's effectual power, received with joy in affliction (v.6).
6:
"And ye became followers of us, and of the Lord, having received the word in much affliction, with joy of the Holy Ghost:" The witnesses note the Thessalonians as imitators, receiving the word with joy in affliction. On "ye became followers of us, and of the Lord," the grammatical voices note the imitation. Barnes and Robertson note that the Thessalonians "became followers (mimetai — imitators) of us, and of the Lord" — they imitated Paul, and through him the Lord himself, copying the pattern of the gospel they had received.

On "having received the word in much affliction," the witnesses note the cost. Barnes and Henry note that they "received the word in much affliction (tribulation, persecution)" — they embraced the gospel amid suffering, for their conversion brought persecution from their countrymen (Acts 17:5-9); they took up the word at great cost.

On "with joy of the Holy Ghost," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the paradox: yet they received it "with joy of the Holy Ghost" — a joy given by the Spirit that triumphed over their affliction; the persecution could not quench the gladness the Spirit wrought. The verse displays the Thessalonians receiving the word with joy in affliction.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Thessalonians "became followers (imitators) of us, and of the Lord," copying Paul and through him the Lord (Barnes, Robertson); "received the word in much affliction (persecution)," embracing the gospel amid suffering at great cost (Barnes, Henry); "with joy of the Holy Ghost," a Spirit-given joy triumphing over affliction (Wesley). The verse displays joy in affliction. The verse marks the imitation: followers of us and the Lord. The verse displays the cost: much affliction. The verse displays the joy: of the Holy Ghost. The verse displays the paradox: joy amid suffering. The imitation displays believers receiving the word gladly under persecution. The verse displays the followers: imitators. The verse displays the affliction: much. The verse displays the joy: Spirit-given. The Thessalonians receiving the word with joy in affliction display the marks of their genuine conversion: they became imitators of Paul and of the Lord, embracing the gospel in the midst of persecution — yet with a joy given by the Holy Ghost that their sufferings could not quench. The verse displays the joyful endurance of the converts, making them a pattern to others (v.7).
7:
"So that ye were ensamples to all that believe in Macedonia and Achaia." The brief verse notes the Thessalonians becoming an example to all the believers. The grammatical voices note the pattern. Barnes and Robertson note the result of their faith and joyful endurance: "ye were ensamples (typoi — patterns, models) to all that believe in Macedonia and Achaia" — their faith was so exemplary that they became a model for all the believers of the two provinces.

On "ensamples," the witnesses note the influence. Barnes and Calvin note that the Thessalonians, themselves recent converts, so shone in faith and constancy that they became a pattern for others to imitate — the imitators (v.6) had become the imitated.

On "Macedonia and Achaia," the grammatical-historical note the reach: their example extended through "Macedonia and Achaia" — the two great provinces of Greece; their reputation spread far beyond their own city. The verse displays the Thessalonians becoming an example to all the believers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the result of their faith — "ye were ensamples (patterns, models) to all that believe in Macedonia and Achaia," a model for all the believers of the two provinces (Barnes, Robertson); recent converts who so shone in faith that the imitators had become the imitated (Barnes, Calvin); their example reaching through both great provinces of Greece (grammatical-historical). The verse displays them as a pattern. The verse marks the example: ensamples. The verse displays the scope: all that believe. The verse displays the reach: Macedonia and Achaia. The verse displays the reversal: imitators imitated. The example displays the converts becoming a model to others. The verse displays the pattern: theirs. The verse displays the believers: all. The verse displays the provinces: two. The Thessalonians becoming an example to all the believers display the power of their faith: themselves recent converts and imitators of Paul, they so shone in faith and constancy that they became a pattern for all the believers in Macedonia and Achaia. The verse displays their exemplary faith, sounding out the word everywhere (v.8). What gives the commendation its weight is that the Thessalonians were themselves but newly come to the faith, converts of only a few weeks or months, who under fierce persecution might have been expected to falter rather than to lead; yet so bright was their constancy and so genuine their joy that the whole body of older believers throughout the two provinces looked to them as the very model of what a believing church should be, the taught become teachers and the followers themselves a pattern to follow.
8:
"For from you sounded out the word of the Lord not only in Macedonia and Achaia, but also in every place your faith to God-ward is spread abroad; so that we need not to speak any thing." The witnesses note the word of the Lord sounding out from them everywhere. On "from you sounded out the word of the Lord," the grammatical voices note the proclamation. Barnes and Robertson note the vivid figure: "from you sounded out (exechetai — rang out, echoed forth, like a trumpet or thunderclap) the word of the Lord" — the gospel resounded from Thessalonica to the surrounding regions; their testimony rang out far and wide.

On "not only in Macedonia and Achaia, but also in every place," the witnesses note the reach. Barnes and Chrysostom note the breadth: not only the two provinces but "every place" — the report of their faith had spread everywhere, far beyond Greece; Thessalonica, a great commercial city, sent the news abroad through its many travellers.

On "your faith to God-ward is spread abroad," the eastern Cyril notes the report: "your faith to God-ward (toward God) is spread abroad" — so widely known was their faith that "we need not to speak any thing"; the fame of their conversion preceded Paul. The verse displays the word of the Lord sounding out from them everywhere.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "from you sounded out (rang out, echoed forth like a trumpet) the word of the Lord," the gospel resounding far and wide (Barnes, Robertson); "not only in Macedonia and Achaia but every place," the report spreading everywhere, sent abroad by the great commercial city (Barnes, Chrysostom); "your faith to God-ward is spread abroad," so known that "we need not speak any thing," their fame preceding Paul (Cyril). The verse displays the word sounding out. The verse marks the sounding: rang forth. The verse displays the reach: every place. The verse displays the faith: spread abroad. The verse displays the fame: needs no telling. The proclamation displays the gospel echoing from them everywhere. The verse displays the word: sounded out. The verse displays the faith: known. The verse displays the spread: wide. The word of the Lord sounding out from them everywhere displays the far-reaching witness of the model church: the gospel rang out from Thessalonica like a trumpet, not only through Macedonia and Achaia but in every place, so that the fame of their faith preceded Paul and needed no telling. The verse displays the spreading report of their faith, as others themselves recount (v.9).
9:
"For they themselves shew of us what manner of entering in we had unto you, and how ye turned to God from idols to serve the living and true God;" The witnesses note their turning from idols to serve the living God. On "they themselves shew of us what manner of entering in we had," the grammatical voices note the report. Barnes and Robertson note that others everywhere "shew (report, tell) of us what manner of entering in (what kind of reception, what success) we had unto you" — the report told both of Paul's effective ministry and of the Thessalonians' response.

On "how ye turned to God from idols," the witnesses note the conversion. Barnes and Calvin note the great change: "ye turned to God from idols" — the Thessalonians, largely Gentiles, abandoned their idol-worship and turned to the true God; conversion is a turning from the false to the real.

On "to serve the living and true God," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the new service: they turned "to serve (douleuein — to serve as bondservants) the living and true God" — over against dead and false idols, they now served the God who lives and is real; conversion issues in a new allegiance and service. The verse displays their turning from idols to serve the living God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: others "shew (report) of us what manner of entering in we had," telling of Paul's effective ministry and the Thessalonians' response (Barnes, Robertson); "ye turned to God from idols," largely Gentile converts abandoning idolatry for the true God (Barnes, Calvin); "to serve the living and true God," over against dead idols, a new allegiance and service (Wesley). The verse displays the turning to God. The verse marks the report: their entering in. The verse displays the conversion: turned from idols. The verse displays the object: the living and true God. The verse displays the service: new allegiance. The conversion displays the Gentiles turning from idols to God. The verse displays the turning: to God. The verse displays the idols: forsaken. The verse displays the service: of the true God. Their turning from idols to serve the living God displays the heart of their conversion: the report told everywhere how the Thessalonians, hearing Paul, turned from their dead idols to the living and true God — abandoning false worship to serve the God who is real. The verse displays the great conversion, completed by waiting for God's Son (v.10).
10:
"And to wait for his Son from heaven, whom he raised from the dead, even Jesus, which delivered us from the wrath to come." The witnesses note the waiting for God's Son from heaven, who delivers from wrath. On "to wait for his Son from heaven," the grammatical voices note the expectation. Barnes and Robertson note the second mark of their conversion (with serving God, v.9): "to wait for (anamenein — await, expect) his Son from heaven" — the Thessalonians lived in expectation of Christ's return; the forward-looking hope marks the believer.

On "whom he raised from the dead, even Jesus," the witnesses note the risen Lord. Barnes and Calvin note the ground of the hope: the Son "whom he (God) raised from the dead, even Jesus" — the resurrection is the pledge that he will come again; the risen Jesus is the one they await.

On "which delivered us from the wrath to come," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the deliverance: Jesus "delivered (rescues, the present-continuing deliverance) us from the wrath to come" — the coming wrath of God's judgment, from which Christ saves his people; their hope is a Saviour who delivers from condemnation. The verse displays the waiting for God's Son who delivers from wrath.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the second mark — "to wait for (await, expect) his Son from heaven," living in expectation of Christ's return (Barnes, Robertson); "whom he raised from the dead, even Jesus," the resurrection the pledge of his coming (Barnes, Calvin); "which delivered (rescues) us from the wrath to come," saving his people from the coming judgment (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays waiting for the Son. The verse marks the expectation: wait for his Son. The verse displays the resurrection: raised from the dead. The verse displays the deliverance: from wrath to come. The verse displays the hope: a Saviour. The waiting displays believers expecting the risen Christ who delivers. The verse displays the Son: awaited. The verse displays the Jesus: risen. The verse displays the deliverance: from wrath. The waiting for God's Son from heaven, who delivers from wrath, displays the forward hope that crowns their conversion: the Thessalonians turned to God to serve him and to await his risen Son Jesus from heaven — the Saviour who delivers his people from the coming wrath of judgment. The verse displays the church's living hope, closing the chapter and opening Paul's account of his ministry (ch.2).
